pattern: A topological order is built by repeatedly emitting a course whose prerequisites are all already placed; tracking in-degrees and a frontier of zero-in-degree courses does this in linear time and exposes a cycle when fewer than all courses get emitted.
hints: Model each prerequisite as a directed edge from the required course to the course that depends on it, then ask in what order the nodes can be laid out so every edge points forward.
Compute each course's in-degree; any course with no unmet prerequisites can be taken now, and taking it removes its outgoing edges, possibly freeing others.
Repeatedly remove a zero-in-degree course and decrement its successors' in-degrees; if you place all of them you have a valid order, otherwise a cycle exists and you return an empty list.

edge_inputs.description: two courses, one prerequisite forces an order
diamond of dependencies with a unique partial structure
a two-node cycle has no valid order, so the answer is empty
a longer cycle is also infeasible
fully independent courses, any order works
a linear chain forces a single valid order

stress.description: random DAG built by only adding edges from lower to higher index so it stays acyclic and a valid order always exists
stress.note: edges always point from a higher-rank to a lower-rank node in a random permutation, guaranteeing acyclicity

stress.description: graphs that sometimes contain a planted cycle so the infeasible branch is exercised
stress.note: about half the cases contain a cycle to test that the reference returns an empty list there
